dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a medical emergency, abdominal pain.
Hi, I'm Alex with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My stomach hurts really bad. I think I tried to hurt myself.
Can you describe where the pain is and how long it's been going on?

I've had a severe stomach pain, about a 10 out of 10, that's stabbing and not going anywhere. It started about an hour ago. I also have a headache that's a 10 out of 10 and has been going on for over a week.
You are awake and oriented to person, place, time, and events.
Your pupils are 3 millimeters, equal, and reactive to light.
I can hear that you're in a lot of pain and emotional distress. Would you like to tell me more about what's going on?
I see you have a medical history of major depression and anxiety. Were you taking your medications?
Yes, but I haven't been taking my Wellbutrin. I drank a bottle of Tylenol, took around 10-12 Aleve pills, and had about five large alcoholic drinks to try to hurt myself.
We noticed you were in a fetal and praying position. Is it more comfortable if we help you onto the stretcher now?
Your airway is self-maintained. You're not having trouble speaking.
Breathing is normal in rate, depth, and effort. Your respiratory rate is 20.
Your pulse is 104, and we have a strong and regular radial pulse.
Your blood pressure is 177 over 82.
Your oxygen saturation is 97%, and your glucose is 116 mg/dL.
Your skin is normal in color, dry, and warm to the touch.
Your breath sounds are equal and clear bilaterally.
Your EKG is showing sinus tachycardia. Have you ever experienced a fast heart rhythm before?
We're going to start an intravenous line in your arm for fluids.
We've placed you on a 4-lead cardiac monitor to evaluate your heart rhythm continuously.
A 12-lead ECG is being done to assess for any abnormalities. You'll feel a few small sticky patches on your chest.
Your gait was assessed as steady. You ambulated a few steps to the stretcher independently.
Your blood pressure has now risen to 187 over 130, and your oxygen saturation has dropped to 83. We'll continue to monitor while providing care.
We're giving you 250 milliliters of normal saline intravenously per protocol. We've also placed a saline lock for potential future use.
Your oxygen saturation improved to 94%, and your blood pressure is 171 over 107.
You'll remain on the cardiac monitor during transport to detect any life-threatening arrhythmias.
We'll cover you with a sheet for your comfort and secure you with a five-point restraint harness during the ride to the hospital.
I'm calling the emergency department to let them know what to expect before we arrive.
We'll obtain another 12-lead ECG at the hospital for detailed evaluation.
Your latest vitals are blood pressure 160 over 70, pulse 87, oxygen saturation 98, and temperature 97.0°F. Your EKG now shows sinus rhythm.
Let's recheck your vitals for accuracy once more.
Your pulse is 87 and regular.
Your breath rate is 20, steady and not labored.
Your blood pressure is 160 over 70 - still within a reasonable range.
Your glucose is currently not measurable.
Your oxygen saturation remains at 98%.
Your EKG is now showing sinus rhythm. No dangerous arrhythmias have been detected.
Let's ask again: how is the pain now? Has it changed?
Patient: It's still a 10. It hasn't changed.
Does anything seem to make it better or worse?
Patient: It feels sharply stabbing and doesn't go anywhere.
There are no signs of pain radiating to any other area.
We continue to monitor for changes. Your report so far is consistent.
It started about an hour ago, correct?
Just to double-check, besides your abdominal pain and severe headache, are there other issues to note?
Patient: No, just the severe pain and emotional crisis.
No known drug allergies on your record.
You ingested Tylenol, Aleve, and alcohol but these are not listed as allergies.
I see from your medical records that you have a history of major depression and anxiety.
You said you had alcohol and Tylenol, but had you eaten anything recently?
Patient: I haven't eaten much. I was suicidal earlier today and that's why I took the pills.
Your temperature is 97.0°F. No other signs suggest a compromised condition.
Your skin remains warm and dry. I don't see rashes or marks right now.
Your neck examinations show no abnormalities or swelling.
No signs of obstruction or drainage from your ears, nose, or mouth.
Your lungs remain clear and equal bilaterally.
Your abdomen is soft and not distended. No acute guarding or tenderness noted.
No urine output reported so far.
Your extremities move well with no deformity or swelling observed.
One last check of your blood pressure. It's stable at 160 over 70.
Your IV line is secure and functioning as expected.
We've already contacted Chippenham Hospital and they are prepared for your arrival.
You will be transferred to the ED bed using the stretcher. No resistance expected.
We'll hand over all your medical information and documentation to the ER team now.
Let me confirm your mental status again - are you still aware of your surroundings and what's happening?
I am. I know where I am and why.
GCS remains at 15: Eye 4, Verbal 5, Motor 6. No observed decline.
No new findings on skin recheck. You remain warm and dry.
No new signs from head and neck recheck.
No respiratory compromises during transport. All sounds remain clear.
Still no guarding or rigidity detected.
No new issues with extremities or back seen.
Final vitals: pulse 87, respirations 20, BP 160/70, O2 sat 98, glucose 0, EKG sinus rhythm.
No distress signs in any system during the ride.
No emergency events occurred during transport. Everything remains stable.
Final check of your pain level before arrival.
It's still a 10. I'm still in so much pain.
And, the events included the feelings of being unwanted and attempts with pills and alcohol?
Yes. I just wanted to end it all, and I wasn't in a good place.
No new signs of side effects from the medications or substances you took.
We are heading to the emergency department now. You're in good hands.
No additional interventions are needed. All necessary steps completed.
You remained stable during the ride. No further action was needed.
The ED team is awaiting - we will arrive within minutes.
Any new issues before arrival at the ED?
No, just the pain and the emotional stress. I hope they can help me.
We are now en route to the hospital. You are secured and on board.
Final vitals are BP 160/70, pulse 87, O2 98%, GCS 15, no new issues.
We expect to arrive at the ED within the next 10 minutes.
All notes, vitals, and interventions are documented and ready for transfer.
You'll be transferred into the hospital bed once you arrive.
Final assessment: no acute distress, condition remains stable.
All systems are steady. No distress reported.
I will communicate the final report once we reach the ED room.
All gear and IVs are accounted for. Patient is ready for handover.
The journey was smooth and incident-free.
We are at the hospital now. ED is aware and in the room.
I'll speak with the ED staff now and transfer care.
Expect to document final readings after full handover.
You are secured and stable for handover.
We followed the medical - abdominal pain (protocol 3 - 2) and medical - overdose (protocol 7 - 1).
All protocol steps have been completed as required per the emergency service protocols.
